cards.front: Can you use an enumerator as an array index directly?
cards.back: Not directly for std::array (it expects std::size_t). You need to cast the enumerator to an integer type using static_cast or std::to_underlying (C++23).

cards.text: In C++23, to convert an enumerator to its underlying value, use {{c1::std::to_underlying()}} from the {{c2::<utility>}} header
To use an enum class value as array index: arr[{{c1::static_cast<std::size_t>}}(MyEnum::Value)]

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Cannot use enum class as array index without casting to std::size_t
cards.explanation: Enum class values don't implicitly convert to integers. Use: arr[static_cast<std::size_t>(Color::red)]

cards.text: To size an array based on an enum, add a {{c1::count}} or {{c2::max}} enumerator as the last value, then use it as the array size

cards.question: How does this pattern ensure the array size matches the enum?
cards.code:
enum class Color { red, green, blue, max };
std::array<int, static_cast<std::size_t>(Color::max)> arr;
cards.answer: The 'max' enumerator automatically equals the count of colors (3), ensuring array size stays in sync with enum values.
cards.explanation: If you add more colors, max automatically increments, keeping the array size correct.

cards.concept: Using enumerations for type-safe array indexing
cards.explanation:
Benefits of using enums for array indices:
  • Type safety: prevents invalid indices
  • Self-documenting: arr[Color::red] is clearer than arr[0]
  • Maintainable: adding enum values automatically updates array size if using count/max pattern
examples: enum class Color { red, green, blue, count };

cards.text: Common naming for the count enumerator: {{c1::count}}, {{c2::max}}, or {{c3::num_items}}

cards.front: Why might you want to specify an underlying type for an enum used with arrays?
cards.back: To ensure the enum values fit within a specific integer type (like std::size_t or int), preventing potential size mismatches or overflow issues.

cards.text: To specify the underlying type of an enum: enum class Color {{c1::: std::size_t}} { red, green, blue };

cards.front: What's a safe way to iterate over array elements using an enum-based index?
cards.back: Use a regular integer loop from 0 to the count enumerator value, casting the loop variable to the enum type when needed. Alternatively, use range-based for loops when you don't need the index.
